skill.name: routeros-netinstall
skill.description: Guidance for planning and running MikroTik RouterOS netinstall-cli workflows, including package selection, etherboot setup, and first-boot scripts.
skill.summary: Plan RouterOS netinstall-cli flashing workflows with package, script, and network guidance.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, sudo, URL, and weak-crypto patterns, but most are Markdown examples or product terms in a documentation-only skill. The confirmed risk is operational: the skill guides privileged RouterOS netinstall workflows that can reformat devices, run first-boot scripts, and download official binaries.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Device Reinstallation Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill documents netinstall-cli workflows that require root privileges and can reformat a RouterOS device. This is legitimate for the domain, but misuse can cause device outage or data loss.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly states that Netinstall reformats the system drive and requires root privileges. The sudo examples are documentation, not hidden execution, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: First-Boot Script Guidance Can Change Device State
medium_findings.description: The skill explains configure scripts and mode scripts that run on a router after installation. These scripts can change device mode and configuration before normal setup completes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantics are clear because the documented -s and -sm options install RouterOS scripts. This is expected netinstall behavior, but it can materially alter device security posture.

medium_findings.title: Official Binary Download and BOOTP/TFTP Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill provides official MikroTik download URLs and examples that run a privileged local BOOTP/TFTP flashing workflow. Users should verify binaries and isolate the install network.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs point to vendor and reference locations, not suspicious endpoints. The workflow still involves privileged network services and binary downloads, which warrants a medium warning.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Detections Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The many command detections occur in inline documentation, tables, and fenced examples. No executable script file or automatic command runner is present in the scanned skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanned file is SKILL.md, and the flagged commands are displayed as usage examples. I found no evidence that the skill executes those commands automatically.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Point to Expected Vendor References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are MikroTik download and documentation references plus a related GitHub wrapper project. No evidence of credential exfiltration or unexpected network destination was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The domains match the documented RouterOS source and related project context. The remaining risk is normal supply-chain verification for downloaded binaries.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography detections appear to match product and option text such as RouterOS, RouterBOOT, and modescript context. No cryptographic algorithm use or implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe RouterOS terms and script behavior, not crypto code. Because only SKILL.md was scanned, there is no implementation path for weak cryptographic use.

dangerous_patterns.title: sudo netinstall-cli Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows sudo netinstall-cli examples that perform RouterOS installation. These commands are expected, but they should be run only on an isolated install host and target device.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact privileged examples are visible in the documentation. They are not malicious by themselves, but they are powerful device-flashing commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: RouterOS Script Installation Options
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents -s and -sm script options for first-boot behavior. These options can automate sensitive configuration and should be reviewed before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file directly documents script installation and execution order. This is legitimate RouterOS behavior with elevated device impact.

content.value_statement: RouterOS reinstalls can fail when package order, boot mode, or scripts are unclear. This skill explains netinstall-cli workflows, flags, package names, and first-boot behavior.
actual_capabilities: Explains official netinstall-cli syntax and option behavior.
Clarifies package ordering and architecture-specific package names.
Compares configure scripts and mode scripts for first boot.
Shows common reinstall, empty configuration, and keep-configuration flows.
Describes etherboot, BOOTP, TFTP, and isolated network requirements.
Summarizes host constraints for x86, ARM Linux, and macOS workflows.
limitations: Does not execute netinstall-cli or flash devices automatically.
Does not verify downloaded MikroTik binaries or package signatures.
Does not replace vendor documentation for model-specific recovery steps.
Does not guarantee data preservation during RouterOS reinstall operations.

use_cases.title: Recover a RouterOS Device
use_cases.description: Plan an isolated netinstall workflow after a failed upgrade, corrupted install, or locked configuration.

use_cases.title: Build Repeatable Flashing Scripts
use_cases.description: Translate netinstall-cli options into a controlled script for consistent lab or staging device preparation.

use_cases.title: Document Deployment Runbooks
use_cases.description: Create clear RouterOS reinstall steps that explain package order, boot mode, and first-boot scripts.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Basic Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a RouterOS netinstall-cli command for one device on eth0 using RouterOS 7.22 arm64 packages. Explain each option.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Reset Modes
prompt_templates.scenario: Operator deciding how much configuration to keep
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare netinstall-cli -r, -e, and no reset flag for a MikroTik router. Include what data may be lost.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare First-Boot Scripts
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a RouterOS netinstall workflow that uses a mode script before the default configuration script. List review checks before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Runbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a production runbook for RouterOS netinstall-cli with package download, isolated network setup, etherboot steps, validation, and rollback notes.

output_examples.input: Plan a netinstall for an arm64 router and keep the old configuration.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step plan covering package selection, isolated Ethernet setup, etherboot entry, a keep-configuration command, and post-install validation checks.

output_examples.input: Explain when to use a mode script instead of a configure script.
output_examples.output: A comparison of execution order, persistence, reboot behavior, and the risks of changing device mode during first boot.

output_examples.input: Create a technician checklist for Netinstall failures.
output: Confirm the device is in etherboot mode.
Use a direct Layer 2 path without competing DHCP services.
Check package architecture and system package order.
Document any data that will not be preserved.

best_practices: Use an isolated Ethernet segment with no competing DHCP service.
Put the RouterOS system package first in every package list.
Review first-boot scripts before running commands with root privileges.
anti_patterns: Running netinstall on a shared production network segment.
Assuming keep-configuration preserves files or application databases.
Mixing package architectures without confirming the target hardware.

faq.question: Does this skill flash routers by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples for netinstall-cli, but it does not execute commands.

faq.question: Why does Netinstall require sudo or root?
faq.answer: Netinstall uses privileged BOOTP, DHCP, and TFTP behavior on the local network interface.

faq.question: Can Netinstall preserve the old RouterOS configuration?
faq.answer: Yes, when neither -r nor -e is used. It does not preserve user files or some application databases.

faq.question: What is the difference between -s and -sm?
faq.answer: -s installs a persistent configure script. -sm installs a one-time mode script that runs first after installation.

faq.question: Which package should appear first?
faq.answer: The RouterOS system package should appear first, followed by optional matching architecture packages.

faq.question: Is this safe for production networks?
faq.answer: Use it carefully. Netinstall should run on an isolated segment because it provides boot services to devices in etherboot mode.
